guide/history.json.title: A Short History of Ireland
guide/history.json.subtitle: From its beginnings to today — 14 eras that shaped it.

items.label: Mesolithic Ireland
items.summary: Hunter-gatherers arrived and made Ireland their home after the ice retreated.
items.text: When the last ice age ended, Ireland's landscape transformed into forests and wetlands, attracting nomadic peoples who crossed from Britain and continental Europe. These early settlers hunted wild boar, red deer, and fish, leaving behind distinctive flint tools and middens of shells at coastal sites. Small bands moved seasonally, following game and gathering nuts, berries, and plants. Evidence from Mount Sandel in County Derry shows some groups may have built semi-permanent camps, suggesting a more complex society than previously imagined.

items.label: Neolithic Revolution
items.summary: Farming families arrived and began building monuments that still stand today.
items.text: Around 4000 BC, Neolithic farmers crossed the Irish Sea with cattle, sheep, and grain, fundamentally transforming the island's landscape as they cleared forests for fields. These communities built communal tombs—megalithic monuments like Newgrange, Knowth, and countless passage graves—that suggest both engineering skill and shared spiritual beliefs. Agriculture was not the only innovation; pottery, polished stone tools, and permanent settlements emerged. Yet many hunter-gatherers likely persisted alongside farmers for centuries, a gradual cultural blending rather than sudden conquest.

items.label: Bronze Age
items.summary: Metalworking arrived, bringing warfare, wealth, and intricate artistic traditions.
items.text: Bronze—an alloy of copper and tin—revolutionized Irish society, and the island became renowned across Europe for its craftspeople and elaborate weapons. Elite warriors emerged, buried in stone-lined cists with golden ornaments, bronze axes, and elaborate decorated shields, signaling a hierarchical society obsessed with status and martial prowess. Gold working flourished, producing delicate torcs, collars, and pendants that showcase remarkable artistic skill. Trade networks stretched to Britain, the Mediterranean, and beyond, bringing new ideas and materials and making Ireland a prosperous hub of the prehistoric Atlantic world.

items.label: Iron Age and the Celts
items.summary: Celtic culture took root, bringing the Irish language, warrior codes, and a new political landscape.
items.text: Celtic peoples—either invading or gradually assimilating—brought iron-working technology, the Irish language, and a fierce warrior culture that would define the island for millennia. Society crystallized into kingdoms and tuatha, small territorial units ruled by kings whose power rested on military strength, patronage, and sacred legitimacy. The distinctive Irish law system, Brehon law, emerged, codifying elaborate rules for everything from honor-price to property rights and kinship obligations. Bards and druids held immense power as keepers of tradition and supernatural knowledge, while the landscape filled with hillforts, ring forts, and the distinctive monuments of Celtic Ireland.

items.label: Early Christian Ireland
items.summary: Christianity spread peacefully, and Ireland became a beacon of learning as Rome fell.
items.text: Saint Patrick's mission in the 5th century converted Ireland to Christianity without the violence seen elsewhere in Europe, perhaps because the lack of Roman infrastructure allowed gradual adoption rather than sudden replacement. Monasteries became the centers of Irish civilization—universities, farms, scriptoriums, and sanctuaries that preserved classical knowledge while much of Europe descended into chaos after Rome's collapse. Monks illuminated manuscripts like the Book of Kells and copied texts from Virgil to Augustine, making Ireland the keeper of Western learning. Remarkably, the monasteries coexisted with the old pagan legal and political systems, creating a unique hybrid culture.

items.label: The Viking Age
items.summary: Norse raiders shattered Ireland's isolation, founding Ireland's first cities.
items.text: Beginning with the raid on Lindisfarne in 793, Vikings turned their attention to Ireland, attacking monasteries for treasure and slaves for two centuries. Unlike the monasteries they raided, these Norsemen established permanent trading posts—Dublin, Waterford, Limerick—that became Ireland's first true cities and gateways to Scandinavian commerce. The raids eventually subsided as Vikings integrated into Irish society, intermarried, and converted to Christianity, becoming indistinguishable from native Irish within a few generations. Yet the Norse legacy endured in place names, trade networks, and a new urban culture that would shape medieval Ireland.

items.label: Gaelic Ireland
items.summary: Irish kings flourished as feudalism swept Europe, creating a golden age of culture and learning.
items.text: With Viking threats fading, Irish Gaelic culture experienced a flowering in the 12th century, as kings patronized poets, scribes, and craftsmen. The high kings of Ireland ruled over an intricate web of provincial kings and local lords, a system formalized in legal texts and mythological genealogies that stretched back to prehistoric times. Monasteries continued to produce manuscripts and maintain schools, and the island's isolation from European feudalism allowed unique political and cultural traditions to persist. Architecture flourished, with the construction of round towers, stone churches, and elaborate stone crosses, creating an instantly recognizable Irish Christian landscape.

items.label: The Norman Conquest
items.summary: Anglo-Norman knights invaded, permanently altering Ireland's political future.
items.text: When the Irish king Diarmait Mac Murchada sought Norman allies to reclaim his throne, he set in motion the conquest of Ireland. In 1170, Richard de Clare, the earl of Pembroke, crossed to Ireland with knights and footmen, establishing a beachhead in Waterford that would transform Irish history forever. The Normans brought feudalism, castle architecture, and a warrior aristocracy that would eventually dominate the eastern half of the island while native Irish remained powerful in the west and northwest. Yet the Normans gradually became 'more Irish than the Irish,' marrying into native families and adopting Irish customs, complicating the very notion of conquest.

items.label: Medieval Ireland Divided
items.summary: The island fractured into Norman and Gaelic realms, each developing distinct identities.
items.text: By the 14th century, the Anglo-Norman colony contracted into the Pale, a fortified zone around Dublin where English law and custom held sway, while Gaelic Irish kings reasserted control over most of the island. The Normans in the Pale grew increasingly English in outlook, speaking English and following English common law, while the Norman lords in the west gradually adopted Irish names, language, and culture. Constant raids, feuds, and small wars characterized medieval Irish life, with the English crown unable or unwilling to invest in reconquest. Yet this division sparked a cultural flowering in both realms, as Gaelic poets celebrated their patrons and Norman lords commissioned castles and manuscripts.

items.label: The Tudor Conquest
items.summary: English kings subdued Ireland through warfare, planted settlers, and imposed Protestant religion.
items.text: Henry VIII's break with Rome set the stage for religious conflict in Ireland, as the English monarchy attempted to impose Protestant Anglicanism on a fiercely Catholic island. A series of brutal military campaigns—particularly the Nine Years' War and its bloody aftermath under Elizabeth I—broke Irish resistance, destroyed the old Gaelic nobility, and murdered or displaced hundreds of thousands. English settlers, or 'planters,' were encouraged to colonize confiscated Irish lands, particularly in Ulster, creating a new Protestant ruling class and deepening sectarian divisions. By 1603, with the Irish defeat at Kinsale, England finally controlled the entire island, though Irish Catholics never accepted Protestant rule.

items.label: Penal Laws and Protestant Ascendancy
items.summary: Catholics were stripped of rights and land while a Protestant elite consolidated power.
items.text: After 1603, Irish Catholics faced a campaign of legal oppression unmatched in severity in Western Europe, barred from voting, owning land above a certain value, and practicing their religion openly. The Penal Laws, enforced over a century, reduced Catholic land ownership from roughly 90 percent to just 5 percent, creating a landless Catholic peasantry dependent on Protestant landlords. Despite these brutal restrictions, Irish Catholic culture endured through hidden priests, family traditions, and folk memory, while a new Anglo-Irish Protestant aristocracy built grand estates and dominated parliament. Famines, rebellions, and religious fervor marked these decades, yet the Protestant Ascendancy seemed unshakable by 1700.

items.label: The Union and the Great Famine
items.summary: Ireland lost its parliament and then lost a million people to hunger and disease.
items.text: In 1801, the Acts of Union merged Ireland into the United Kingdom, abolishing the Dublin parliament and ending even the pretense of Irish autonomy. Then, in 1845, potato blight struck, triggering the Great Famine that killed roughly one million people and drove another million to emigrate, primarily to America, draining Ireland of its youth and strength. The famine exposed and accelerated the injustice of Irish landlessness and British indifference, fueling nationalist sentiment and shaping the Irish diaspora worldwide. By 1870, Ireland's population had halved, but Irish Americans were organizing political power across the Atlantic, and at home, a new generation demanded independence.

items.label: Independence and Civil War
items.summary: Ireland fought for independence, achieved it, then tore itself apart.
items.text: The Easter Rising of 1916, a rebellion that initially angered many Dubliners, transformed Irish politics when the British executed its leaders, making martyrs of men who had seemed foolish. Sinn Féin swept elections, and a guerrilla war from 1919 to 1921 forced Britain to negotiate, resulting in Irish independence in 1922—though partition created the separate, predominantly Protestant state of Northern Ireland. The new Irish Free State descended into brutal civil war as republicans rejected the partition, leaving scars that haunted Irish politics for generations. By 1950, an independent Ireland was a Catholic-dominated, economically backward, rural country, isolated from Europe and struggling to reimagine itself.

items.label: Modern Ireland
items.summary: Economic transformation and cultural change made Ireland one of Europe's success stories.
items.text: Ireland joined the European Economic Community in 1973, breaking centuries of isolation and beginning a transformation that would accelerate in the 1990s as American tech companies made Dublin their European hub. The 'Celtic Tiger' boom of the 1990s and 2000s brought wealth, though financial collapse in 2008 revealed its fragility and left scars that lingered for years. Simultaneously, Irish society underwent radical secularization—contraception was legalized, divorce permitted, same-sex marriage approved by referendum in 2015—reversing the Catholic Church's total dominance of Irish life. Today's Ireland, a wealthy EU member with a global reputation for innovation and culture, bears little resemblance to the poor, rural, priest-ridden country of 1950, though its history remains vivid in memory and landscape.

guide/food.json.title: Food & Drink
guide/food.json.subtitle: How to eat well in Ireland — customs, dishes and where to go.

items.label: Espresso culture is newer here
items.text: Ireland has embraced quality coffee relatively recently, so expect excellent espresso in Dublin, Cork, and Galway, but instant coffee still appears in smaller towns and rural pubs. If you're particular about your coffee, stick to independent cafés rather than chains.

items.label: Tea is still king
items.text: Irlandans drink far more tea than coffee, and they take it seriously—usually Barry's or Lyon's brand, milky and hot. If offered tea, accept it; it's a genuine gesture of hospitality.

items.label: 3fe Coffee (Dublin)
items.text: A third-wave coffee roastery in Dublin's Smithfield with single-origin beans and skilled baristas. Genuinely excellent and worth the queue on weekends.

items.label: Badger & Dodo (Cork)
items.text: A cosy independent café serving specialty coffee and homemade pastries in a converted Victorian building. Popular with locals and a good escape from tourist areas.

items.label: Colcannon
items.text: Mashed potatoes mixed with shredded cabbage or kale, butter, and scallions. It's humble comfort food, best served with a fried egg on top and a knob of butter melting into it.

items.label: Boxty
items.text: A potato pancake from Northern Ireland and the border counties, made with grated raw potato and flour, fried until crispy. Order it filled with meat stew or smoked salmon for a proper meal.

items.label: Seafood chowder
items.text: A creamy soup loaded with local fish, mussels, and prawns. Kinsale in Cork is famous for it, and it's best eaten in a waterfront pub with brown bread.

items.label: Bacon & cabbage
items.text: Often mistaken for corned beef and cabbage (an Irish-American invention), the Irish version uses boiled bacon rashers with white cabbage and parsley sauce. It's the real St. Brigid's Day dish.

items.label: Soda bread
items.text: Dense, slightly sweet bread made with baking soda instead of yeast, best eaten warm with butter. The brown soda bread is denser and more common than the white version.

items.label: Black pudding
items.text: A breakfast staple made from pork blood, offal, and oatmeal. Don't be squeamish—it's rich, slightly sweet, and delicious fried alongside eggs and bacon at a full Irish breakfast.

items.label: Pub culture (the real thing)
items.text: Pubs are the heart of Irish dining and socializing. Look for neighborhood pubs with locals playing cards or dominoes—these serve honest, affordable food and feel authentic. Avoid Temple Bar in Dublin and similar tourist pubs unless you want loud crowds and premium prices.

items.label: Michelin-starred restaurants in Dublin
items.text: Dublin has serious fine dining: Eleven Forty One, Éclat, and Sole all hold Michelin stars. Book ahead and expect creative modern Irish cuisine at London-equivalent prices.

items.label: Lunch is better value than dinner
items.text: Many restaurants offer excellent set lunch menus at half the dinner price. Eating your main meal at lunch and having lighter fare in the evening is both budget-friendly and culturally normal.

items.label: Food markets and delis
items.text: English Market in Cork and Temple Bar Food Market in Dublin sell fresh produce, cheese, smoked fish, and ready-made meals. Grab lunch here to eat like a local and avoid restaurant markup.

items.label: Avoid tourist-trap restaurants
items.text: Stay away from places with picture menus, those advertising 'authentic Irish food' in multiple languages, and anything within two blocks of major monuments. The best meals are in neighborhood spots where Irish people actually eat.

items.label: Rashers, rashers, rashers
items.text: A 'rasher' is a slice of bacon (closer to back bacon than American crispy bacon). 'Bacon & eggs' on an Irish menu means rashers, not American-style strips.

items.label: Chips are fries
items.text: When you see 'chips' on an Irish menu, it means french fries. 'Crisps' are what Americans call potato chips. A 'spice burger' is a flattened burger served on a bun—order it in a chipper (fish & chips shop), not a restaurant.

items.label: Coddle, stew, and hotpot
items.text: 'Coddle' (Dublin) is layered potatoes and sausages, 'Irish stew' is mutton and potatoes, and 'hotpot' varies by region. These are all hearty, one-dish meals perfect for cold weather.

items.label: Brown bread comes with everything
items.text: Restaurants automatically serve brown soda bread with soups and chowders. It's included, not a separate charge. Butter it generously.

items.label: Full Irish breakfast terms
items.text: 'Brekkie' or 'fry' means the cooked breakfast with rashers, sausages, black pudding, eggs, tomato, and mushrooms. 'Continental' means toast and pastries. Always ask for 'brown bread' if you want it instead of white.

items.label: Guinness is the default
items.text: Guinness is ubiquitous and genuinely better in Dublin (especially at the Guinness Storehouse or any pub near the brewery). However, Irish craft beer has exploded—try Galway Hooker, Smithwicks, or local breweries like Eight Degrees in Cork.

items.label: A pint takes time to pour
items.text: Guinness and other stouts are poured slowly in two stages to settle. Don't rush the bartender or ask them to speed up. This is ritual and part of the experience.

items.label: Irish whiskey renaissance
items.text: Irish whiskey production has exploded in the past decade. Jameson is the most famous, but try Redbreast, Green Spot, or Bushmills for something more interesting. Whiskey is drunk neat or with a splash of water, rarely with ice.

items.label: Wine is imported and pricey
items.text: Ireland doesn't produce wine, so imported bottles are expensive. Order wine by the glass rather than the bottle unless you're splurging. Beer or whiskey offer better value.

items.label: Irish coffee in its birthplace
items.text: Foynes Flying Boat Museum café and Dromoland Castle both claim to serve the original Irish coffee (whiskey, coffee, brown sugar, cream). It's best drunk slowly after dinner, not as an afternoon pick-me-up.

items.label: Closing times are real
items.text: Pubs close at 11:30 PM on weeknights and 12:30 AM on weekends. There's no 'last call'—the bar just stops serving. Plan accordingly.

items.label: Reservations are essential in cities
items.text: Dublin, Cork, and Galway restaurants, especially at dinner, require advance booking. Walk-ins may wait 90 minutes or be turned away. Rural pubs rarely need reservations.

items.label: Tipping is 10–15%, not mandatory
items.text: Tipping is appreciated but not required in Ireland. For good service, leave 10–15% or round up. Tip less in casual pubs, more in restaurants. Card machines often ask if you want to add a tip—you can decline.

items.label: Meal timing is different
items.text: 'Dinner' means the evening meal (7–9 PM), 'lunch' is midday, and 'breakfast' is morning. If someone invites you for 'tea,' they usually mean an early evening meal around 5–6 PM, not hot tea with pastries.

items.label: Pub etiquette: order at the bar
items.text: In casual pubs, order and pay at the bar—there's rarely table service unless it's a restaurant-pub hybrid. Sitting down doesn't mean someone will come take your order. Exception: upscale restaurants have table service.

items.label: Conversation is currency
items.text: In pubs, brief chats with strangers are normal and welcome. A simple 'How's the day treating you?' can lead to recommendations and local knowledge. Don't be shy.

items.label: Sunday roast is sacred
items.text: Many Irish families eat a roast dinner (usually beef, chicken, or lamb with potatoes and veg) on Sunday afternoons. Restaurants are busier on Sundays for this reason. Booking is essential.

items.label: Supermarkets for budget eating
items.text: Dunnes Stores, Tesco, and Lidl are everywhere and cheap. Buy lunch items, snacks, and breakfast here rather than at cafés. Their deli counters also make fresh sandwiches for under €6.

items.label: Dietary restrictions are understood
items.text: Vegetarian and vegan options are widely available in cities and tourist areas. Coeliac (gluten-free) restaurants exist in Dublin and Cork. Tell your server upfront; Irish restaurants are accommodating.

items.label: Breakfast is served late
items.text: Most cafés don't open until 8–9 AM, and breakfast is served until 11 AM or later. There's no American-style early breakfast culture. Plan accordingly if you're an early riser.

guide/transport.json.title: Getting Around
guide/transport.json.subtitle: Trains, roads, taxis and how to move around Ireland.

items.label: Book ahead for savings
items.text: Irish Rail (irishrail.ie) offers significant discounts on advance bookings, especially for intercity routes like Dublin–Cork or Dublin–Galway. Booking 2–4 weeks ahead can cut fares by 30–50%.

items.label: Use the Irish Rail app
items.text: The official Irish Rail app lets you check live times, book tickets, and receive delay alerts. Mobile tickets work on most routes, though some services still require printed tickets.
Strikes and delays are common, especially on weekends and bank holidays. Always check for service updates on irishrail.ie before traveling and allow extra time for connections.

items.label: Consider rail passes
items.text: The Irish Railpass (3–15 days) offers unlimited travel on Irish Rail and Translink (Northern Ireland). It's worth buying if you plan 3+ intercity journeys within a week.

sections.title: City Buses & Metro [Luas, Bus Éireann, Dublin Bus]

items.label: Luas tram in Dublin
items.text: Dublin's Luas (luas.ie) has two lines (Red and Green) covering the city center, Tallaght, and Connolly. It's fast, reliable, and runs every 5–10 minutes during peak hours.

items.label: Get a Leap card
items.text: Buy a Leap card (€5) at newsagents or Dublin Bus stops. It works on all Dublin and Cork buses, Luas, and Irish Rail commuter services, offering 20% savings over cash fares.
Dublin Bus and Bus Éireann often run late or skip stops during rush hour. Have a backup plan (Luas, taxi, or walk) if time-sensitive, especially in peak evening commute (5–7 pm).

items.label: Regional buses cover the island
items.text: Bus Éireann and GoBus operate intercity and rural routes cheaply. Book online at buseireann.ie or gobus.ie for best prices; fares are typically €10–25 for long distances.

items.label: No contactless or card payment on buses
items.text: Most Irish buses require exact cash, Leap cards, or pre-purchased tickets. Few accept contactless debit/credit cards; always carry cash or a Leap card.

sections.title: Taxis & Ride Apps [Uber, Free Now, local cabs]

items.label: Uber and Free Now are reliable
items.text: Uber operates in Dublin, Cork, and Galway. Free Now (formerly Hailo) covers most Irish cities and works like Uber; both apps show fares upfront and are safer than hailing on the street.

items.label: Unlicensed taxis are a risk
items.text: Never take an unmarked taxi or accept rides from unlicensed drivers offering transport on the street. Always use official Uber, Free Now, or regulated taxi firms displayed in hotels/apps.

items.label: Licensed black cabs are fixed-rate in cities
items.text: In Dublin, licensed black cabs charge set rates from the airport and main stations; fares are regulated. Always ask the fare before boarding if no meter is visible.
Surge pricing on Uber and Free Now is steep during peak hours (Friday–Saturday nights, 11 pm–3 am). Budget 30–40% extra or use public transport if available.

items.label: Tipping is optional but appreciated
items.text: Rounding up to the nearest euro or adding 5–10% is customary in cities, but never obligatory. Uber and Free Now apps include optional tipping screens.

items.label: Drive on the left; international license required
items.text: Ireland drives on the left. You must carry your driving license (IDP recommended if non-EU), passport, and proof of insurance. Rental firms require you to be 21+ (25+ for premium cars).

items.label: Speed camera fines are automatic and heavy
items.text: Speeding fines start at €80 and can reach €1,000+ if 20+ km/h over the limit. Speed cameras are frequent on motorways and main roads; speed limits are 50 (town), 80 (regional), 100 (national), 120 (motorway) km/h.

items.label: M50 tolls (Dublin) and eFlow
items.text: The M50 motorway around Dublin charges €2.10 per crossing (€1.80 off-peak). Pay via eFlow.ie by midnight the same day or face €3 surcharge + fine. Rental cars have auto-tolls; check your bill.

items.label: Single-track roads and narrow country lanes
items.text: Rural Ireland has narrow, potholed roads with poor lighting and no centerline markings. Avoid driving at night; sheep and cattle may block roads. Drive slowly and pull over to let locals pass.

items.label: Fuel is expensive; book cars with full tank
items.text: Petrol costs €1.30–1.50/L; diesel €1.20–1.40/L. Book rental cars 'full-to-full' to avoid inflated refueling charges at the agency. Plan long drives with stops; petrol stations in remote areas may be 50+ km apart.

items.label: Parking restrictions and resident zones
items.text: Most city centers require paid parking (€1.50–4/hour) via apps like PayPark or Easypark. Many areas are residents-only after 6 pm; check signs carefully to avoid €60+ fines.

items.label: Dublin Airport is the main hub
items.text: Dublin Airport (daa.ie) is 15 km north of the city. The Aircoach bus (€7–8) runs every 10 min to the city center (25–40 min); taxis cost €20–30 and often hit traffic.

items.label: Use Aircoach or Irish Rail from Dublin
items.text: Aircoach (aircoach.ie) is cheaper and faster than buses. Irish Rail Dart Express (€10–12) runs directly to Connolly Station in 13 minutes; book online or at machines to save 20%.

items.label: Regional airports are small but efficient
items.text: Cork, Shannon, and Knock are 15–30 km from city centers. Buses are cheap (€5–8) but less frequent; taxis are €15–25. Rental cars are available but book ahead during peak season (June–August).

items.label: Airport parking scams and overpricing
items.text: On-airport parking costs €3–5 per hour; pre-book online to save 30%. Off-airport 'meet and greet' services often overcharge; stick to official DAA or verified airport operators.

items.label: Check baggage allowances early
items.text: Ryanair charges €10–15 for checked bags and often has strict carry-on limits. Weigh and measure baggage before arriving; overweight fines are €50–100 per bag.

items.label: Dublin & Dún Laoghaire to UK/France
items.text: Irish Ferries and Stena Line run car ferries from Dublin to Holyhead (Wales, 3 hours) and from Dún Laoghaire to Wales. Book on irishferries.com or stenaline.ie; fares vary €50–200 depending on season.

items.label: Rosslare to France and Wales
items.text: Rosslare Harbour (southeast Ireland) offers direct ferries to France (Cherbourg, Roscoff) and Wales via Irish Ferries and Stena Line. Useful if exploring the south or avoiding Dublin traffic.

items.label: Book multi-journey car passes
items.text: Frequent travelers can buy multi-journey passes (4–10 crossings) at 10–20% discount. Vehicle and foot passenger prices are bundled; book online for instant discounts.
Ferry schedules are seasonal and weather-dependent. Winter sailings (November–March) may be delayed or cancelled by storms; always check 24 hours before departure and have flexible plans.

items.label: Arrive 2 hours early for cars, 1 hour for foot passengers
items.text: Security and check-in are strict; arriving late may result in missing your sailing. International travel documents (passport) are required.

sections.title: Tickets & Passes [Leap, Irish Railpass, Hop-on Hop-off]

items.label: Leap card is essential for Dublin
items.text: A Leap card (€5, reloadable) covers Dublin Bus, Luas, Dart, and Irish Rail commuter services at 20% cheaper rates than cash. Load €20–50 and use for all trips; available at newsagents and online.

items.label: Irish Railpass for multi-day travel
items.text: The Irish Railpass (€99 for 3 days, €149 for 5 days over 15 days) covers unlimited Irish Rail and Translink travel. Worth buying if planning 3+ intercity journeys; book at irishrail.ie or buy at Dublin stations.

items.label: Weekly bus passes in cities
items.text: Cork, Galway, and Limerick offer weekly bus passes (€15–20) for unlimited city transport. Buy at bus stations or on the operator's app (CorkBus.ie, GalwayBus.ie, etc.).

items.label: Hop-on Hop-off tourist buses
items.text: Dublin, Cork, and Galway have hop-on hop-off double-decker buses (€20–30/day) covering major sights with narrated commentary. Great for first-time visitors; book online for discounts.

items.label: Day passes don't always cover peak hours
items.text: Some city day passes exclude rush-hour travel (7–9 am, 5–7 pm). Read the terms carefully before buying; peak-hour premiums apply on certain routes.

items.label: Buy a local SIM at the airport
items.text: Vodafone, Three, and Eir sell pay-as-you-go SIMs (€10–20) at Dublin Airport newsagents and convenience stores. Data plans (10 GB for €20–30) are much cheaper than roaming.

items.label: Three Ireland offers best-value data
items.text: Three (three.ie) offers 10–20 GB prepaid plans for €20–30/month, the cheapest in Ireland. Vodafone and Eir are pricier but have better rural coverage. Check signal maps before choosing.

items.label: Rural areas have patchy coverage
items.text: 4G is reliable in cities but gaps exist in west/southwest Ireland and mountains. Download offline maps (Google Maps, Maps.me) before traveling to remote areas; 3G fallback is slow.

items.label: EU roaming included with Irish SIM
items.text: Your Irish SIM includes EU roaming at no extra cost (calls, texts, data). Useful if day-tripping to Northern Ireland (part of UK, roaming may apply—check your plan).

items.label: WiFi is widely available in cities and towns
items.text: Hotels, pubs, cafes, and bus stations offer free WiFi. Eir WiFi (€5–10/day) covers public hotspots nationwide, useful as backup if your SIM data runs out.
